text:
St Mary's Hospital 

Institute of Neurology 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Hughes  
Riverside Medical Practice  
12 Albert Road, London SE1 7AB  

Dear Dr Hughes,  

Sophie Turner, DOB: 14-05-1994, Hospital No: N482031 NHS No. 9081764523  
Flat 3, 21 Brook Street, London W1K 4HA 

Medication: 

Sodium Valproate 500 mg morning / 1000 mg night, continued. Levetiracetam 250 mg twice daily, newly initiated as adjunct. 

I reviewed this patient with known generalised epilepsy. She reports good adherence to medication and confirms she is currently not driving by self-report. She had no seizures for nearly a year following initiation of Valproate, then developed myoclonic jerks leading to a tonic seizure two Saturdays ago. The event occurred on waking after a week of shift work and reduced sleep, and she notes brief morning myoclonic jerks on the preceding two days. There was no associated intercurrent illness, alcohol use, or missed doses reported. 

Neurological examination today is normal. Prior EEG (2023) showed generalised spike–wave discharges consistent with a generalised epilepsy syndrome; MRI brain (2023) was unremarkable. In view of the breakthrough myoclonus and a single tonic seizure on an otherwise stable Valproate regimen, we discussed strategies to reduce sleep deprivation and stress. As she prefers to avoid further upward titration of Valproate due to mild weight gain, we have commenced low-dose Levetiracetam with a plan to titrate cautiously as tolerated. She understands to report any mood changes. 

Safety and lifestyle: She remains seizure-aware and continues to avoid driving. We reinforced general safety advice, including supervised bathing, caution with heights, and regular sleep patterns. She will contact us promptly if further myoclonic clusters or any generalised tonic–clonic seizures occur. 

Plan: 
- Continue Valproate 500 mg AM / 1000 mg PM.  
- Start Levetiracetam 250 mg twice daily for 2 weeks; if myoclonus persists, increase to 500 mg twice daily after discussion by telephone.  
- Bloods (LFTs, FBC) in 6 weeks given ongoing Valproate.  
- Seizure diary to monitor frequency and potential triggers; provide update via clinic portal in 4 weeks.  
- Driving: patient already not driving; advised to remain off driving and follow DVLA guidance after any further events.  

We will arrange follow-up in three months, sooner if there are recurrent myoclonic jerks or any further generalised tonic–clonic seizures. 

Yours sincerely

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter states the patient had no seizures for nearly a year after starting Valproate, then had a single tonic seizure two Saturdays before the clinic date. Myoclonic jerks occurred on the two preceding days but their ongoing frequency is not quantified. The patient is not currently seizure-free (last event was two Saturdays ago), so we estimate frequency over the past year as approximately 1 event per year.
